FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 5.1; U.S. sample of 4289 adolescents from 21 recruitment sites, ages 11-14 at follow-up; described as 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (release 5.1)' throughout methods.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-lagged panel models (CLPMs) and hierarchical mixed-effects models (HMEMs) run on ABCD data across 3 waves to examine directional associations between psychopathology and gaming disorder.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in JAMA Network Open; no indication of being a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific estimate exists anywhere in the paper. The sole screen-related variable is the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), which measures gaming disorder. No time-on-phone, SM use, texting, named platform, MPIQ, SMAQ, or other qualifying phone/SM variable appears in any table, supplementary material, or sensitivity analysis. Step 2 not reached. Per binding clarification, gaming-disorder scales (VGAQ, unspecified-platform) fail IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable, which is absent here.

notes: Paper is entirely about psychopathology <-> gaming disorder (VGAQ) longitudinal associations. No smartphone or social media variable of any kind is analyzed.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 5.1; U.S. sample of 3419 adolescents; 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14); explicitly described as 'US-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study.'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear mixed models for prospective associations of parental factors with sleep outcomes, plus mediation and moderation analyses, using ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open; no indication of being a duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen use (total daily screen time from the Youth Screen Time Survey) appears only as a mediator between parental factors (warmth, monitoring, conflict, psychopathology) and adolescent sleep outcomes. In every mediation model (Figure 2, mediation tables), screen use is strictly the intermediate variable (path A = parental factor -> screen use; path B = screen use -> sleep). It is never the IV or DV in a stand-alone inferential analysis. Per binding clarification §7, SM/screen as mediator-only fails IC2. No phone/SM-specific estimate (β or otherwise) appears as IV or DV anywhere in the paper, including supplementary sensitivity analyses.

notes: Screen use functions solely as a mediator in all analyses. No qualifying phone/SM variable appears as IV or DV in any inferential model.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data release 5.1; U.S. sample of 4754 adolescents recruited from 21 sites at baseline ages 9-10; explicitly described as 'Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (Release 5.1).'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: independent linear mixed-effects regression models testing individual-difference predictors of screen time (ST) and problematic screen use (PSU) across video games, social media, and smartphones.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAACAP Open; no indication of being a duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Table 3 reports separate β coefficients for individual-difference predictors (urgency, reward sensitivity, externalizing, general cognitive ability, EF, learning/memory) on 'Social media hours' (average daily social media ST) as DV — e.g., urgency β = +.06, p < .001; externalizing β = +.04, p = .008. Table 4 reports β for predictors of SMAQ scores (e.g., urgency β = +.05, p < .001; punishment sensitivity β = +.04, p < .001) and MPIQ scores (e.g., urgency β = +.10, p < .001; externalizing β = +.08, p < .001) as DVs. Social media hours, SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire) are all qualifying phone/SM-specific variables as DV. The other side includes individual-trait predictors (urgency/impulsivity, psychopathology, motivation, cognitive ability) — all qualifying individual-trait constructs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Tables 3 and 4 report β coefficients, SE, and p-values for all predictor-outcome pairs; inferential analysis with quantitative results is present throughout.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported in Tables 3 and 4 for the qualifying SM/phone variables (social media hours, SMAQ, MPIQ) paired with individual-trait predictors.

FT_EC7_reason: All three EC7 conditions hold: (a) design is longitudinal — baseline individual differences (ages 9-10) predicting year-4 outcomes (ages 12-15); (b) SM/phone variables (social media hours, SMAQ, MPIQ) are the DV in every analysis; (c) no analysis exists anywhere in the paper where SM/phone is the IV with an individual-trait DV. The zero-order correlation matrix (Table 2) is descriptive only, not inferential. No cross-sectional inferential analysis with SM as DV or IV is reported, so the cross-sectional carve-out does not apply.
notes: Paper examines whether baseline personality, motivation, psychopathology, and cognition predict later screen time and PSU. Social media hours and SMAQ/MPIQ are always the outcomes (DV) in all inferential models. EC7 applies: longitudinal SM-as-DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis.
